category: AMMO-67 HAZMAT Familiarization & Safety in Transport
description: AMMO-67-DL course covering DOT hazard classes, proper shipping names, UN/NA numbers, placarding, shipping papers, emergency response, 49 CFR compliance, and military hazardous materials transportation requirements.

questions.q: What are the three types of hazmat transportation?
questions.a: Land (highway/rail), sea, and air.

questions.q: What regulation governs the transportation of hazardous materials in the United States?
questions.a: 49 CFR (Code of Federal Regulations), specifically Title 49 — Transportation.

questions.q: What is the definition of a hazardous material (HAZMAT)?
questions.a: A substance or material capable of posing an unreasonable risk to health, safety, and property when transported in commerce, as designated by the Secretary of Transportation.

questions.q: How many DOT hazard classes are there?
questions.a: Nine hazard classes.

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 1?
questions.a: Explosives — substances and articles that have a mass explosion hazard, projection hazard, fire hazard, or minor blast hazard.

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 2?
questions.a: Gases — including flammable gases (2.1), non-flammable compressed gases (2.2), and poisonous/toxic gases (2.3).

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 3?
questions.a: Flammable liquids — liquids with a flash point of not more than 60 degrees Celsius (140 degrees Fahrenheit).

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 4?
questions.a: Flammable solids (4.1), spontaneously combustible materials (4.2), and materials dangerous when wet (4.3).

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 5?
questions.a: Oxidizers (5.1) and organic peroxides (5.2).

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 6?
questions.a: Toxic/poisonous substances (6.1) and infectious substances (6.2).

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 7?
questions.a: Radioactive materials.

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 8?
questions.a: Corrosive materials — substances that cause visible destruction or irreversible damage to living tissue or metal.

questions.q: What is DOT Hazard Class 9?
questions.a: Miscellaneous hazardous materials — materials that present a hazard during transport but do not fit into any other hazard class (e.g., dry ice, magnetized materials, elevated temperature materials).

questions.q: What is a placard?
questions.a: A diamond-shaped sign displayed on all four sides of a transport vehicle or railcar to communicate the hazard class of the materials being transported to first responders.

questions.q: Where must placards be displayed on a transport vehicle?
questions.a: On all four sides — front, back, and both sides of the vehicle or railcar.

questions.q: Can more than one placard be displayed on a vehicle?
questions.a: Yes, more than one placard can be displayed if the vehicle contains materials from different hazard classes.

questions.q: What is the purpose of shipping papers?
questions.a: To provide a basic description identifying the material's hazard class, proper shipping name, UN/NA identification number, and quantity being transported.

questions.q: What is DD Form 2890?
questions.a: DOD Multimodal Dangerous Goods Declaration — the shipping paper used by the military for transporting hazardous materials by military or commercial vehicles.

questions.q: What four elements make up the basic description on hazmat shipping papers?
questions.a: Identification number (UN/NA), proper shipping name, hazard class and division, and packing group.

questions.q: What is the Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG)?
questions.a: A DOT publication that advises first responders (firefighters, police, EMS) on appropriate initial emergency response procedures for hazardous materials incidents.

questions.q: What do the yellow-bordered pages in the ERG contain?
questions.a: A numerical index of four-digit UN/NA identification numbers cross-referenced to guide numbers.

questions.q: What do the blue-bordered pages in the ERG contain?
questions.a: An alphabetical index of materials by proper shipping name, cross-referenced to guide numbers.

questions.q: What do the orange-bordered pages in the ERG contain?
questions.a: Safety recommendations for fire, explosion, and health exposure, organized by guide number.

questions.q: What do the green-bordered pages in the ERG contain?
questions.a: Initial isolation and protective action distances for materials that are highlighted in the yellow and blue pages.

questions.q: What do the white pages in the ERG contain?
questions.a: Directions for using the ERG, general information on hazardous materials, and emergency response procedures.

questions.q: What does the marking 'RQ' on a package indicate?
questions.a: Reportable Quantity — the material is a hazardous substance at or above the quantity that must be reported if released into the environment.

questions.q: What is DD Form 626 used for?
questions.a: Motor Vehicle Inspection (Transporting Hazardous Materials) — used by QASAS or vehicle inspectors to verify driver documentation and inspect vehicles before and after loading with HAZMAT.

questions.q: When is a vehicle inspected during hazmat transport operations?
questions.a: Three times: before loading, after loading, and before unloading.

questions.q: What is a proper shipping name (PSN)?
questions.a: The standardized name listed in the Hazardous Materials Table (49 CFR 172.101) used to describe a hazardous material on shipping documents.

questions.q: What are UN/NA identification numbers?
questions.a: Four-digit numbers assigned to hazardous materials: UN numbers are internationally recognized, NA numbers are used only in North America. They appear on shipping papers, packages, and placards.

questions.q: What is a Safety Data Sheet (SDS)?
questions.a: A document that provides detailed information about a hazardous material's properties, health hazards, protective measures, safety precautions, and emergency procedures.

questions.q: What is the Joint Hazard Classification System (JHCS)?
questions.a: A DOD system that provides hazard classification data sheets for military ammunition and explosives items.

questions.q: What documents must accompany a hazmat shipment?
questions.a: DD Form 626, shipping papers (DD Form 2890), SDS or JHCS data sheet, a copy of the applicable ERG guide page, and 49 CFR Section 397.3 (Driving and Parking Rules).

questions.q: What are compatibility groups in hazmat transport?
questions.a: Letter designations (A through S) that identify which ammunition and explosive items can be safely transported together.

questions.q: What label indicates a mass explosion hazard?
questions.a: Hazard Division 1.1 label — an orange diamond with an exploding bomb symbol and the number 1.

questions.q: What are the four types of required HAZMAT training per 49 CFR 172.704?
questions.a: General awareness/familiarization, function-specific, safety, and security awareness training.

questions.q: What is the purpose of 49 CFR Section 397.3?
questions.a: It establishes driving and parking rules for vehicles transporting hazardous materials, including requirements for attended vehicles, safe havens, and routing.

questions.q: What is a 'HAZMAT employee' according to 49 CFR?
questions.a: Any person who directly affects hazardous materials transportation safety, including those who load, unload, handle, prepare for transport, manufacture, test, recondition, or transport hazardous materials.

questions.q: How often must HAZMAT employees receive refresher training?
questions.a: At least once every three years, per 49 CFR 172.704(c)(2).

questions.q: What are packing groups and what do they indicate?
questions.a: Packing Groups I, II, and III indicate the degree of danger: PG I = great danger, PG II = medium danger, PG III = minor danger.

questions.q: What is the placard for Class 9 miscellaneous hazardous materials?
questions.a: A white diamond with black vertical stripes on the upper half and the number 9 on the lower portion.

questions.q: What markings are required on packages containing hazardous materials per MIL-STD-129?
questions.a: Proper shipping name, UN/NA identification number, hazard class labels, weight, consignee and consignor information, and any special handling instructions.

questions.q: What is the Hazardous Materials Table?
questions.a: A table in 49 CFR 172.101 that lists hazardous materials along with their proper shipping names, hazard classes, identification numbers, packing groups, labels, and special provisions.
